title: 5 Signs Your Website Is Costing You Customers
excerpt: If your site looks outdated on mobile or takes forever to load, you are losing business every day. Here is what to watch for.
author: Shubham Sunny

sections.heading: Your site is your first salesperson
paragraphs: Most small business owners invest in signs, ads, or referrals — then ignore the one place every stranger checks first: your website. If it looks outdated, loads slowly, or breaks on phones, you lose trust before anyone picks up the phone.
After 150+ projects, these are the five patterns I see most often — and they cost real money every week.

sections.heading: 1. It looks wrong on a phone
paragraphs: Open your site on your phone right now. Can you read the menu or services without zooming? Is the phone number visible without scrolling? If not, roughly 80% of your Google traffic is having the same problem.
Fix: mobile-first layout, readable text size, sticky call button.

sections.heading: 2. It takes more than 3 seconds to load
paragraphs: People leave slow sites. Google ranks them lower too. Old WordPress themes, uncompressed images, and cheap hosting are the usual culprits.
Fix: modern static build, WebP images, proper hosting — often a full refresh is cheaper than patching a 2015 theme forever.

sections.heading: 3. Visitors cannot tell what you do in 5 seconds
paragraphs: If someone lands on your homepage and thinks “what is this company?” — they leave. Clear headline, one photo of your work, and a single obvious next step (call, book, quote).

sections.heading: 4. No proof you are real
paragraphs: Missing address, no photos of your team or work, generic stock images, no reviews — all signal “maybe scam.” Local businesses need local trust cues: real photos, Google reviews, hours, insurance logos where relevant.

sections.heading: 5. Contact is buried
paragraphs: Forms that go nowhere, phone numbers only in the footer, no WhatsApp — every extra click kills leads. Put contact where thumbs reach on mobile.

If two or more of these sound familiar, a modernization project usually pays for itself within weeks of extra calls or bookings. We offer a free review — no pitch, just an honest look at what is costing you customers.

title: What Mobile-Friendly Actually Means
excerpt: No jargon — just what mobile-friendly means for your business and why it matters for US customers.
author: Shubham Sunny

sections.heading: Not just “it shrinks on a phone”
paragraphs: Mobile-friendly does not mean your desktop site gets tiny. It means the layout, buttons, and text were designed for a small touch screen first — then scaled up for desktop.
Google uses mobile-first indexing: it primarily looks at your mobile version when deciding how to rank you.

sections.heading: What good looks like
paragraphs: Text you can read without pinching. Buttons big enough for a thumb. No horizontal scrolling. Images that load fast on cellular data. Forms that work with autofill.
Test on an older Android phone on 4G, not just your latest iPhone on WiFi — that is what many of your customers use.

sections.heading: Why US customers care
paragraphs: US consumers search on phones during lunch breaks, in parking lots, between meetings. If your competitor’s site loads in 2 seconds and yours takes 8, they call your competitor.
Mobile-friendly is not a nice-to-have for restaurants, dentists, contractors, and local services — it is the main storefront.

title: How Long Does a Website Redesign Take?
excerpt: A realistic timeline for small business website projects — from first call to launch.
author: Shubham Sunny

sections.heading: Typical timeline: 2–4 weeks
paragraphs: For a small business site (5–8 pages), most projects land between two and four weeks from kickoff to launch. Not because we drag — because you need time to approve design and send photos.

sections.heading: Week 1 — Discovery & plan
paragraphs: We review your current site, list what is costing leads, collect content (services, photos, hours), and agree on sitemap. You get a clear scope and fixed price before build starts.

sections.heading: Week 2 — Design
paragraphs: Mockups for homepage and one inner page. Usually two revision rounds. Goal: you can show it to a colleague and they immediately understand what you do.

sections.heading: Week 3 — Build & content
paragraphs: Development, mobile QA, forms, analytics, speed tuning. We enter your content or help you write plain-English copy that converts.

sections.heading: Week 4 — Launch
paragraphs: DNS, SSL, final device testing, handoff doc. Optional: we stay on for small updates the first month.
Rush jobs are possible for simpler sites; complex e-commerce or custom features need longer — we always give a realistic date upfront, not a surprise delay later.

title: When Does Data Scraping Actually Make Sense?
excerpt: Stop copy-pasting from competitor sites — here is when automation pays for itself (and when it does not).
author: Shubham Sunny

sections.heading: Copy-paste is not a strategy
paragraphs: If someone on your team spends hours every week copying prices, listings, or leads from websites into Excel — that is a signal scraping might pay for itself in days.
Scraping means automated collection of public data: competitor prices, product catalogs, job listings, reviews — delivered on a schedule to CSV, JSON, or your database.

sections.heading: When it is worth it
paragraphs: You check the same sites repeatedly. Data goes stale before you finish a category. You need alerts when something changes. Manual work does not scale past a few dozen items.
When it is not: one-time lookup of ten URLs — just do it manually. We will tell you honestly if automation is overkill.

sections.heading: Legal & ethical basics
paragraphs: We only collect publicly visible data — no bypassing logins or paywalls. Every project starts with a scope review so you know exactly what is collected and how it will be used.

title: What It's Like Working With Us
excerpt: Eight years and 163+ projects in — here's how we actually work, from first message to handoff.
author: Shubham Sunny

sections.heading: Eight years in
paragraphs: I started on Fiverr in 2018 doing website fixes and small builds. Over time that turned into a small studio — same core people, same way of working. We are not a 200-person agency. We are a founder-led team that has shipped 163+ projects for clients in the US, UK, India, and the Middle East.

sections.heading: How a typical project runs
paragraphs: First message → short call to understand the goal and budget. We send a clear scope and timeline before any payment. During the build you get updates in plain English — mockups to approve, weekly demos on bigger apps, QA on real phones before launch.
At handoff you get logins, a short guide, and direct contact info. Many clients come back months later for phase two — that is the relationship we care about.

sections.heading: When we are not the right fit
paragraphs: If you need the absolute cheapest quote with no discovery call, we are probably not it. If scope changes every day with no timeline adjustment, projects stall — we will say that upfront.
If you want a reliable partner who answers messages, documents handoff, and has been doing this for eight years — that is us.

title: WhatsApp Bots for Local Business — Worth It?
excerpt: If customers message you on WhatsApp at 9 PM, a structured bot beats a messy inbox every time.
author: Shubham Sunny

sections.heading: Your customers already use WhatsApp
paragraphs: For restaurants, clinics, and local shops in many markets, WhatsApp is the default — not email, not contact forms. If you answer manually at 9 PM, you lose orders. If you ignore messages, you lose trust.

sections.heading: What a bot actually does
paragraphs: Structured flow: menu → cart → address → payment link. Or: book appointment → pick service → confirm time. No more “which item?” back-and-forth during rush hour.
We have built bots that cut order errors by 90% for cloud kitchens and cut response time from hours to under two minutes for agencies.

sections.heading: Build vs generic widget
paragraphs: Generic chat widgets dump text into an inbox. Custom bots qualify, calculate, and push to your CRM or kitchen screen. Cost starts higher but ROI shows up in week one if volume is there.
